coredata.dc:title: MnCo2O4/N-doped graphene quantum dot vigorously coupled to MXene nanosheet: A bifunctional Oxygen electrocatalyst outperforms Pt/IrO2 benchmark electrocatalysts in metal-air batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Faraji, Monireh
Yousefzadeh, Samira
Nassar, Maadh Fawzi
Zahra, Musaddak Maher Abdul
coredata.dc:description: The rational design and fabrication of a high performance, durable, and cost effective bifunctional electrocatalyst employed for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is of remarkably great importance for the commercialization of rechargeable metal–air batteries. While electrocatalysts designed based on spinel oxides have been deemed as promising catalyst materials for the ORR and OER, their catalytic performance still must be considerably enhanced to satisfy the prerequisites of practical applications. In this work, a heterostructure (MnCo2O4/N-GQD/MXene) of Ti3C2Tx (MXene) nanosheets decorated with spinel manganese-cobalt oxide (MnCo2O4) nanoparticles and N-doped-graphene quantum dots(N-GQD) fabricated via facile hydrothermal method. The presence of plentiful active reaction sites, high surface area and distinctive electronic structure, resulting from robust interfacial interaction, lead to the higher electron conductivity and faster ORR/OER kinetics in Zn-air batteries. Therefore, the obtained MnCo2O4/N-GQD/MXene electrocatalyst presenting an outstanding ORR performance with a low over potential of only 22 mV, half-wave potential (E1/2) of 0.87 V (vs RHE), and remarkable long-term durability, strikingly outperforms commercial Pt/C (20 % w/w). Concerning OER, the MnCo2O4/ N-GQD/MXene exhibiting an onset potential of 1.54 V(vs RHE) and a Tafel slope of 65 mV dec−1 displays better performance than IrO2. The zinc-air battery with MnCo2O4/ N-GQD/MXene cathode shows superior peak power density (152.8 mW cm−2), tremendous capacity efficiency up to 753 mAh g−1 and substantial stability, proving that MnCo2O4/ N-GQD/MXene could feasibly be used in metal–air batteries.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rational design and fabrication of a high performance, durable, and cost effective bifunctional electrocatalyst employed for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is of remarkably great importance for the commercialization of rechargeable metal–air batteries. While electrocatalysts designed based on spinel oxides have been deemed as promising catalyst materials for the ORR and OER, their catalytic performance still must be considerably enhanced to satisfy the prerequisites of practical applications. In this work, a heterostructure (MnCo2O4/N-GQD/MXene) of Ti3C2Tx (MXene) nanosheets decorated with spinel manganese-cobalt oxide (MnCo2O4) nanoparticles and N-doped-graphene quantum dots(N-GQD) fabricated via facile hydrothermal method. The presence of plentiful active reaction sites, high surface area and distinctive electronic structure, resulting from robust interfacial interaction, lead to the higher electron conductivity and faster ORR/OER kinetics in Zn-air batteries. Therefore, the obtained MnCo2O4/N-GQD/MXene electrocatalyst presenting an outstanding ORR performance with a low over potential of only 22 mV, half-wave potential (E1/2) of 0.87 V (vs RHE), and remarkable long-term durability, strikingly outperforms commercial Pt/C (20 % w/w). Concerning OER, the MnCo2O4/ N-GQD/MXene exhibiting an onset potential of 1.54 V(vs RHE) and a Tafel slope of 65 mV dec−1 displays better performance than IrO2. The zinc-air battery with MnCo2O4/ N-GQD/MXene cathode shows superior peak power density (152.8 mW cm−2), tremendous capacity efficiency up to 753 mAh g−1 and substantial stability, proving that MnCo2O4/ N-GQD/MXene could feasibly be used in metal–air batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The globally growing request for energy, associated with the dwindling of fossil fuels and environmentally destructive effect, has inspired extreme research interest on green and renewable energy production and storage technology [1]. Oxygen electrochemistry is of great significance in some of these breakthrough renewable energy devices such as fuel cells, metal-air batteries, and water splitting systems used in hydrogen generation [2,3]. Nevertheless, the efficiency and performance of these devices are strongly limited by the slowly struggling kinetics of oxygen evolution reaction (OER)and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) [3,4].

On that account, fabricating of highly competent electrocatalysts for ORR and/or OER can be considered as a crucial step for development of these devices. In general, catalysts fabricated based on noble metals such as Pt and (Ir/Ru) have been employed to accelerate the kinetics of ORR and OER, respectively. Nonetheless, the high cost and low-resources of these catalyst materials impede commercially wide applications of fuel cells and metal–air batteries [5,6].

A proper bifunctional electrocatalyst for ORR and OER ought to encompass numerous metal centers which have efficiently functional sites with multiple redox-active centers carrying out both ORR and OER via four-electron route. Recently, several cathode electrocatalysts have been discovered to raise the performance of metal-air batteries and fuel cells, such as metal alloys [7–10], carbon based materials [11–15], non-oxide materials, and transition-metal oxides [16,17]. Some of the transition metal oxide are active for ORR while others are proper catalysts for OER [18,19]. For example, nickel and cobalt have showed acceptable activity toward OER whereas Mn and Fe are considered as suitable catalysts for ORR. Mixed-valent transition-metal oxides (spinel’s) like Co3O4, NiCo2O, MnO2, MnCo2O4, can be deemed as the high-performance bifunctional electrocatalysts due to high abundancy, facile fabrication process and great stability in alkaline media [20–22]. In addition, their spinel structure offers adjustable valence states leading to bifunctionality toward ORR/OER and easily tunable structure [23]. In spinel metal oxide family, Co-based group such as Co3O4 are the most studied catalysts [24–27]. These oxide materials acting as donor-acceptor locations for oxygenated groups and catalyze OER and ORR via adsorption/desorption mechanism. In Cobalt based oxides, CoII atoms, located in tetrahedral sites, are able to catalyze ORR while CoIII atoms are considered as active sites for OER. Nevertheless, the high price, lower durability and environmental problems with Co3O4 inhibit its commercialization as an electrode material for batteries. In order to enhance the conductivity of Co3O4 as well as making it inexpensive and eliminating environmental impact, plenty of researches have been done by substituting one of the Co2+ions which results in fabrication of MCo2O4 (M = Mn, Ni, Mg and Cu)[28]. Numerous works have been reported on mixed-metal Co3O4 based spinel oxides such as MnCo2O4 [29–33], Ni2Co2O4 [34–36] ZnCo2O4 [37]. Specially, MnCo2O4 is the one which has been widely employed as an ORR and OER electrocatalyst in metal-air battries and fuel cells. Wang et al. fabricated a MnCo2O4 hybrid mesoporous structure mainly composed of MnIV covered the surface, MnIII found in bulk and CoII found in both surface and bulk. Thanks to MnIV and CoII abundant surface, the prepared catalyst presented high activity of Co3O4 toward OER and Mn2O3 toward ORR.The potential difference between ORR and OER reached as small as ΔE= 0.83 V [38].

However, some measurements should be taken to overcome the poor electrical conductivity, surface area and surface activity of the MnCo2O4 to enhance its catalytic activity [31,39]. Mostly, altering the catalyst’s electronic structure will result in enhanced catalytic activity originating from optimized adsorption energy of the reaction species on the surface [40]. Electronically-engineered catalyst structures could be formed by incorporation of a heteroatom, making structure defects, and controlling the anions in the composition which creates various active sites [41,42]. Moreover, fabricating hybrid structures and grafting the catalyst material to different substrates as well as bringing to contact of reactants to various phases could be considered as helpful approaches to modifying the electronic structure which directly affecting the catalytic performance. Another criterion having strong impact on oxygen bifunctional catalyst performance is morphology exerting influence on mass diffusion and charge transfer.

In this work, we have taken multistep steps to modify the catalytic activity of MnCo2O4 over OER and ORR. Firstly, newly emerged two dimensional transition metal carbides (MXene’s), possessing excellent electrical conductivity, low adsorption energy and diffusion barrier, have been used as conductive catalyst support materials to facilitate the electron transfer kinetics [43,44]. Secondly, regarding that the ion transport pathways can be shortened effectively by making the particle size smaller, MnCo2O4 nanopaticles have been prepared and loaded on MXene nanosheets. Owing to their distinctive structures and mechanical features, MXenes are employed in various kinds of batteries to preserve the integrity of the electrode configuration and increase the durability of the battery's function [45]. Finally, N-doped graphene quantum (NGQD) dots have been added to hybrid catalyst system as the catalytic activity of N-doped carbon materials toward ORR are proved. In addition, the presence of NGQD significantly could improve the charge transfer rate in the hybrid structure.

The fabrication of layered Ti3C2 MXene nanosheets was adopted from our previous work [46]. Typically, 2 g Ti3AlC2 was gradually added into 20 ml of 6 M HF multiple times, and vigorously mixed with a magnetic stirrer for 48 h at 50 ◦C. Afterward, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and washed with deionized distilled water until to a pH of 7. To finish, the resulted layered MXene product was dried in a vacuum oven during the night.

The N-GQD was prepared according to previously reported literature [47]. Facile hydrothermal method was employed for preparation of N-GQD. Briefly, 0.5 g Citric acid (as the carbon precursor), 0.32 g Urea (as the N source) were dissolved into 10 ml deionized water (100 ◦C) and kept stirring for 2 h, after that the reaction completed in a 200 ml autoclave at 155 °C for 12 h. The yielded product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 7 min to throw away the solid residue and get the aqueous solution to use in the next step. The resulted product was dialyzed with a dialysis membrane for 24 h. The obtained solution consists of N-GQD.

The MnCo2O4/ N-GQD/MXene hybrid structures were synthesized through a hydrothermal process. Initially, the 12 ml Co(OAc)2 (0.05 M) was dispersed in a solution containing (60 % w/40 % w) of ethylene glycol and deionized water, afterward mixed under vigorous stirring for 2 h and added 6 ml of Mn (OAc)2 (0.05 M) and mixed for 1 h, to get a mixture with a molar ratio of 2:1Co/Mn. the prepared MXene (200 mg) and N-GQD (100 mg) were dispersed in the prepared solution and blended for 1 h, then the blend was moved into a Teflon sealed autoclave and heated to 185 ◦C for 14 h. Reaching to room temperature, the obtained product was centrifuged and washed with the mixture of ethanol and deionized water severally. Next, the resulted precipitate heated in a vacuum oven at 85 ◦C for 12 h.The overall fabrication procedure of MnCo2O4 / N-GQD /MXene composites is shown in Scheme 1.

To study the crystal structure of the prepared materials, X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained by a diffractometer with Cu-Ka radiation(λ = 1.5410 Å) with a step size of 0.05° (conventional θ–2θ diffractometer). Raman studies were performed at 532 nm by using a Renishaw inVia Plus Micro-Raman spectroscopy system provided with a 50 mW DPSS laser. The surface morphology and properties of the fabricated samples were investigated by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a spectrometer with a radiation source of Al Kɑ to examine the surface compositions of the synthesized nanocomposites. The curve fitting of the core-level XPS lines and Elemental composition were performed employing the CasaXPS software with a Gaussian–Lorentzian product function and a non-linear Shirley background subtraction. The peaks were fitted using the element library in CasaXPS [48]. The specific surface areas of the composite materials were obtained by employing the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, while the related pore size distribution was gained by Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method.

Electrochemical OER/ORR studies were done in KOH solution (0.1 M) on Auto Lab equipment utilizing the three-electrode system. The working electrode was prepared by mixing of 5 mg prepared catalyst nanocomposite with 1 ml isopropanol/Nafion (5 % Nafion) solution under ultra-sonication for 20 min. Afterward, 18 µL of the resulted catalyst ink was spry-coated onto a glassy carbon electrode with 5 mm diameter. The prepared electrode was dried in an 80 ◦C oven for 2 h. The saturated Ag/AgCl electrode and standard coil platinum wire were put to use as a reference and counter electrode, respectively. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were acquired in KOH aqueous solution (0.1 M) at the sweep rate of 50 and 10 mV s−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of MnCo2O4 QD/ N-GQD /MXene nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern of Ti3AlC2 (MAX) phase and Ti3C2Tx)MXene(, shown in Fig. 1a, confirmed successful preparation of MXene following the elimination of Al atoms from Ti3AlC2 phase. This could be verified with the extremely reduced intensity of (002) plane’s peak and its relocation to lower 2Ɵ (9.9° to 8. 9°) [49]. The XRD pattern of as prepared MAX phase is consistent with the ICDD No:00–052–0875 [50]. Subjected to HF etching and treated with ultrasonic which is followed by exfoliation of layers, the MAX phase almost losses its sharp peaks at 33–43° [51] Therefore, there can be seen a remarkable decrease in the peaks’ intensity, particularly, the most sharp peak of (104) plane. XRD pattern of NQD (Fig. 1b) consists of a broad peak at 24.9◦, matching to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [52]. The obtained diffraction peaks of MnCo2O4 are in good agreement with the standard ICDD pattern which is related to the spinel MnCo2O4 (PDF #23–1237). Several characteristic peaks located at 18.7◦,30.6◦, 36.1◦, 43.6◦ are respectively attributed to the (111), (220), (311), (400) planes [32]. The observed peaks for the MnCo2O4 QD/ N-GQD /MXene composite are consistent with the major diffraction peaks of N-GQD, MXene and MnCo2O4, denoting that the heterostructure of MnCo2O4 QD/ N-GQD /MXene has been successfully fabricated.

So as to additional approve the existence state of Mxene nanosheets, N-GQD and MnCo2O4 in the prepared hybrid structure, Raman spectra of synthesized samples are obtained and shown in Fig. 1c. Raman spectrum of MnCo2O4 /N-GQD /MXene consists of the key characteristic peaks from Mxene, N-GQD and MnCo2O4, confirming the effective combination of MXene, N-GQD and MnCo2O4 components. The peak observed at 197 cm−1 is ascribed to the features of tetrahedral positions (CoO4), corresponding to the F2 g symmetry [53]. The peaks seen at 490, 531 and 695 cm−1 are attributed to Eg, F2 g and A1 g symmetry, respectively [53,54]. Co3+ and Co2+ species located at tetrahedral and octahedral sites are corresponding for observed phonon symmetries [55]. The signals attributed to the Mxene structure are seen at 199 cm -1and 716 cm -1 and originate from the out of plane vibrations (A1 g) of Ti and C atoms. The peaks which are related to the in-plane modes (A1 g) of Ti, C and surface terminal groups are placed at 283, 365 and 624 cm -1, respectively [49,56,57]. They evidently show that MnCo2O4 and MXene preserved their inherent structure. In addition, the characteristic peaks associated with the graphitic structures of N-GQD are emerged at 1328 cm -1 (D) and 1594 cm -1 (G) and triggered by vibration or defect of the C–C bond mostly at the edges of planes and the vibration of CC (sp2), respectively [58] . The relocation of these peaks indicates that there is a vigorous interaction among N-GQD, MXene and MnCo2O4.

SEM images of as-synthesized MXene and MnCo2O4 / N-GQD /MXene composite are shown in the Fig. 2. Fig. 2a and b demonstrate that MXene is successfully delaminated and has a particular accordion-like layered morphology, in which the layers are evidently detached from each other. This lamellar structure, with several microns lateral size, results in active surface area in the prepared composite catalyst. As can be evidently seen in Fig. 2c, the MnCo2O4 and N-GQD nanoparticles, formed via hydrothermal process, are homogeneously loaded on the surface and between the layers of the MXene nanosheets. Furthermore, the formation of MnCo2O4 inhibits the restacking of MXene layers. In addition, the porous structure originating from the stacking of MnCo2O4 offers plentiful active sites and improves the fast transfer of electrolyte ions. In view of the small size of N-GQD, their microstructure was only investigated by TEM imaging. TEM analysis of fabricated N-GQD (Fig. 2d) revealed that the N-GQD particles have formed in uniform size. The average diameter was about approximately 5 nm. Additionally, the high-resolution TEM image, shown in Fig. 2d, display that the N-GQD particles have 0.21 nm lattice fringes which is properly resolved. This is in accordance with the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene, signifying that several N-GQD particles are made of about one to three graphene layers. TEM and HRTEM (Fig. 2e and f) studies of MnCo2O4/N-GQD/MXene further disclose that MnCo2O4 nanoparticles are formed in average diameter of 5–6 nm on MXene nanosheets indicating that growth on MXene nanosheet leads to smaller size of MnCo2O4 nanoparticles in comparison to the bulk MnCo2O4 crystal. The lattice spacing of the grown MnCo2O4 nanoparticles were estimated to be 0.221 nm attributed to the (200) face. The layer distance of MXene phase was determined to be about 0.26 nm ascribing to the (1 0 0) face of Ti3C2.

XPS examinations were performed to investigate the elemental composition of the fabricated composites. As seen in XPS survey spectrum ( Fig. 3a), C, N, Ti, Co, Mn, and O are the main elements composing the MnCo2O4 /N-GQD /MXene hybrid structure. According to the elemental analysis, the atomic ratio of Mn to Co is determined 1:1.7, which is in fine consistency with the theoretical atomic fraction (1:2). When N-GQD is incorporated into the MXene, a new peak at the binding energy of 399.5 eV, assigned to N 1 s, emerged in XPS spectrum of the prepared composite. The fading of F element in the MnCo2O4 /N-GQD /MXene composite is due to the hydrothermal process. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ti 2p, seen in Fig. 3b, for the MnCo2O4 /N-GQD /MXene composite could be deconvoluted into six peaks. The signals at about 454.3 and 461.8 eV attributed to C-Ti-Tx. The peaks at 456.5 and 463.4 eV are apportioned to the TiO2 and signifying the surface oxidation through hydrothermal process. The peaks shown at 458.2 and 464.3 eV match to Ti-O2-X [59,60]. In Fig. 3c, the C 1 s spectrum of the MnCo2O4 /N-GQD /MXene composite shows six peaks at 290.5 eV (π-π * ), 289.2 eV (C-OOH), 286.9 eV (CO), 285.3 eV (C-N), 284.6 eV (CC), and 281.65 eV (Ti-C). The newly emerged C-N peak found at 285.5 eV indicates that N element is introduced in the carbon framework [61]. The N 1 s core level XPS spectra could be split up to four essential peaks (Fig. 3d): 398.5 eV (N-6), 400.6 eV (N-5), 401.5 eV (N-Q), and 403.2 eV(N-X). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Mn 2p in MnCo2O4 /N-GQD /MXene composite (Fig. 3e) could be split into four peaks, consisting of the Mn3+ 2p1/2 peak at 654.9 eV, the Mn2+ 2p1/2 peak at 652.92 eV, the Mn3+2p3/2 peak at 643.7 eV, and the Mn2+ 2p3/2 peak at 641.9 eV. These peaks further confirm the presence of mixed valences (Mn3+and Mn2+) in the spinel MnCo2O4 structure [31,62]. In the same way, the XPS spectrum of Co 2p (Fig. 3f) could be fitted by bearing in mind two spin-orbit doublet features of Co2+ and Co3+ and two prominent shake up satellites. The 780.9 eV (Co 2p3/2) and 795.5 eV (Co 2p1/2) could be ascribed to the Co3+, whereas the 783.1 eV (Co 2p3/2) and 797.1 eV (Co 2p1/2) can be accredited to the Co2+ [29,63].

With the aim of the study of the pore structure and calculate the specific surface area of the prepared materials brunauer-emmett-teller (BET) investigations were carried out by N2 adsorption/desorption ( Fig. 4a). The specific surface area of pristine MnCo2O4,MXene and N-GQD are obtained 35.510, 51.210 75.31om2/g,respectively while MnCo2O4 /N-GQD /MXene possess a high specific surface area of 420.521 m2 g−1, which is around ten order of magnitude of pure MXene.The high surface area of the prepared composite indicates that MnCo2O4 and N-GQD prevent MXene nanosheets’s aggregation [50]. What can be inferred from Pore size distributions diagram is (Fig. 4b) that MnCo2O4 /N-GQD /MXene consists of copious mesopores facilitating mass transfer process over the catalyst surface.

With the intention of study the electrocatalytic potency of the fabricated catalysts, CV experiments were first carried out with a glassy carbon rotating disk electrode (RDE) under O2 saturated atmosphere in a 0.1 M KOH electrolyte. The obtained CV plots of the electrodes are seen in the Fig. 5a. All samples show apparent O2 reduction peak (Ep) proving the catalytic activity of the prepared catalysts toward the ORR. It could be seen that the Ep belonging to the pristine MXene emerges in the lowest potential while the Ep of the MnCo2O4 /N-GQD /MXene catalyst is observed in the most positive potential even than that of the benchmark Pt/C catalyst. The kinetics function of the electrodes was examined by LSV analysis in the O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH. The onset potential (Eonst), half wave potential, proportional to half of the maximum current density, (E1/2), and Tofel slop which are indicators for electrodes activity are extracted from LSV plots (Fig. 5b) for prepared electrodes and are seen in Table S1. The synthesized MnCo2O4 /N-GQD /MXene hybrid catalyst presents highest Eonse, E1/2, Jk (at E = 0.85 V) and the lowest Tofel slope signifying the better electrocatalytic activity of the MnCo2O4 /N-GQD /MXene. These results imply that MnCo2O4 /N-GQD /MXene shows better catalytic performance for ORR than the commercial Pt/C, pristine MnCo2O4, MXene and most electrocatalysts reported in the literature. As seen in Fig. 5a MnCo2O4/MXene shows EOCV (0.968 V vs RHE), E1/2 (0.832 V vs RHE) and Tafel slop (72 mV dec−1) indicating that the MnCo2O4/MXene presents better performance than pristine MXene, MnCo2O4 and N-GQD. This means that MXene has improved the electron transfer kinetics of MnCo2O4 leading to higher catalytic activity[24,50]. The superior catalytic activity demonstrated by MnCo2O4/N-GQD/MXene reveals that there is a synergy between MnCo2O4 and N-GQD.

In order to get more insight on MXene/MnCo2O4/N-GQD electrode’s kinetic parameters, LSV plots were obtained at various rotation rates and depicted in the Fig. 5d. The ORR current density slowly enhanced with increasing rotation rate originating from the faster mass transport rate near the electrode surface [64,65]. Based on the Fig. 5b, the related Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plot was acquired in the Fig. 5e. The K-L plots are linearly proportional with first-order reaction kinetics. The obtained electron transfer number of 3.97 designating a quasi 4e- oxygen reduction reaction process. Furthermore, chronoamperometry measurement was carried out for 300 h and MXene/MnCo2O4/ N-GQD catalyst preserved 94 % of its initial current whereas the Pt/C catalyst only retains 83 % of the relative current after 300 h. Thus, it could be inferred that the MXene/MnCo2O4/ N-GQD catalyst has a superior ORR durability in alkaline environment.

The electrocatalytic activity of prepared samples as well as commercial benchmark catalysts (IrO2 and Pt/C) was examined toward OER utilizing a RDE in an O2-saturated electrolyte (0.1 M KOH). The potential in which current density reaches to 10 mA cm−2 (E10) was commonly considered as the OER overpotential and used to evaluate the OER performance of an electrocatalyst [66]. As depicted in the Fig. 6a, MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene exhibits a lower overpotential (Ej10 = 1.54 V) than IrO2 (1.56 V), N-GQD (1.63 V), Pt/C (1.62 V) and MXene (1.75 V) at the current density of 10 mAcm−2 (j10). Tafel plots can shed light on reaction kinetics of studied electrodes toward the OER. As observed in the Fig. 6b, the value of Tafel slope for MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene, IrO2, N-GQD, Pt/C, MnCo2O4 and MXene are 65, 70, 92, 90, 81, 98 mV dec−1, respectively. As an intensive parameter, Tafel slope intrinsically depends on the rate determining step of the reaction. The lower Tafel slope for MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene specifies the faster electron transfer between the electrocatalyst and electrolyte [67]. The excellent OER performance of the prepared composite catalysts can be attributed to the presence of Co2+ and Co3+ along with N-doped graphene quantum dot and conductive MXene platform [68]. In the next step, the bifunctional activity of MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene was evaluated toward ORR and OER and was weigh the differences between that of the combined Pt/C and IrO2. As shown in the Fig. 6c, the observed potential difference (0.84 V) of the prepared catalyst between Ej10 and E1/2 was slighter than that of the benchmark catalyst composite (Pt/C and RuO2) (0.92 V), displaying that MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene is a capable electrocatalysts for the reversible ORR and OER in rechargeable Zinc-air batteries.

The durability is a remarkable criterion that must be well-thought-out to develop the practical application of an electrocatalysts. The chronoamperometry study for 24 h was done in 0.1 M KOH to evaluate the OER stability of MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene and compare with the commercial IrO2 (Fig. 6d). The test was done at the fixed potential of 1.60 V vs RHE. The as-prepared MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene displays an outstanding OER activity with only 14.5 % current decays, whereas IrO2 catalyst experiences a notable deterioration in relative current around 40 %. Then, the activity of fabricated bifunctional MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene, was investigated toward OER/ORR and was compared to that of a blend of IrO2 and Pt/C. As depicted in the Fig. 6c, the potential difference (0.85 V) of the prepared composite electrocatalysts between Ej10 and E1/2 was narrower than that of the blended benchmark catalysts (0.91 V), denoting that the MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene could be an auspicious catalyst for the reversible ORR and OER in metal-air batteries. The electrocatalytic performance of this composite toward ORR and OER is compared (Table S2) with peer MXene-based oxygen bifunctional electrocatalysts reported in the literatures.

The excellent bifunctional catalytic activity of MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene hybrid structure toward ORR/OER persuaded us to consider the potentiality for practical utilization. At first, we constructed a home-made Zn-air battery with the MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene heterostructure as an air-cathode, a zinc plate as an anode and 6 molL−1 KOH /02 molL−1 zinc acetate as the electrolyte. As shown in the Fig. 7a, the battery displays an open-circuit potential (OCP) of 1.461 V above 100 min, which is enhanced in comparison with the prepared battery based on the commercial benchmark catalysts (Pt/C + IrO2/C)(OCP= 1.351 V)., the maximum power density, illustrated in the Fig. 7b, obtained for the MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene based Zn-air battery gets to 152.8 mW cm−2which is higher than that of the Pt/C + IrO2/C peer (133.5 mW cm−2). Notably, there is no voltage loss during the long discharge time as can be seen in the Fig. 7c. Furthermore, the specific capacity of MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene based battery, which is standardized to the amount of the used Zn, was obtained (753 mAh gZn−1) at j = 10 mA cm−2, as seen in, higher than that of Pt/C-IrO2 based battery (722 mAh gZn−1). Besides, the ZAB with MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene presents more steady charging-discharging performance and a less voltage gap in comparison to the ZAB with commercial catalysts (Pt/C-IrO2) at j = 10 mAcm−1 (Fig. 7d). In order to study the durability of fabricated ZAB, which is a crucial indication for practical application, the galvanostatic charge-discharge plots were obtained at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 for 4 h each cycle. As depicted in Fig. 7e, the ZAB with MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene shows a smaller charge-discharge gap from 0.92 to 0.94 V after 300 h. This result is indicative of superior charge-discharge behavior and stability of MnCo2O4/ NGQD/MXene bifunctional ORR/OER electrocatalyst.

In summary, MnCo2O4/ N-GQD nanoparticles, decorated on conducive 2D MXene nanosheet electrocatalyst was successfully synthesized by a facile hydrothermal process. Reaping benefits of the high surface area, extraordinary porous structure and great electron conductivity, providing plenty of active sites and a boundless number of channels for electron/ion transfer and electrolyte diffusion, the obtained MnCo2O4/ N-GQD/MXene catalyst demonstrates fabulous bifunctional catalytic activity and durability toward both the OER and ORR with a ΔE of 0.85 V. The fabricated rechargeable zinc-air battery with MnCo2O4/ N-GQD/MXene displays a higher power density and a cycling efficiency, which is higher than those of the battery with Pt/C-IrO2 catalyst. This work may offer some stimulus for the design and fabrication of non-precious metal catalysts for Zn-air batteries.

Monireh Faraji: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Samira Yousefzadeh: Investigation, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Maadh Fawzi Nassar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Musaddak Maher Abdul Zahra: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.
